title: Why Does Cooperation Collapse Even When Everyone Benefits?

challenges.stem: Two competing SaaS companies are locked in a pricing war. Both have cut margins to near-zero. Both CEOs privately admit they'd profit more if prices went back up. Neither raises prices first. What structure is preventing cooperation?

options.text: It's a prisoner's dilemma — whoever raises prices first loses customers to the one who doesn't
Both CEOs are irrational and driven by ego
The market is truly zero-sum so cooperation is impossible
They haven't tried communicating their intentions clearly enough

challenges.explanation: This is a classic prisoner's dilemma. Mutual cooperation (both raise prices) is optimal, but unilateral cooperation (you raise, they don't) is the worst outcome for the one who moves first. Communication alone doesn't fix it — they need structural changes like long-term signaling or simultaneous action.

challenges.stem: During the Cuban Missile Crisis, Kennedy's military advisors pushed for immediate air strikes despite knowing it could trigger nuclear war. What force made 'defection' (striking first) feel rational to them?

options.text: Fear — being the one who cooperates while the other defects means maximum loss
Ego — they wanted to prove American military superiority
Status — they wanted recognition for decisive action
Identity — they saw themselves as warriors, not diplomats

challenges.explanation: The core force in any prisoner's dilemma is fear of the 'sucker's payoff' — cooperating while the other side defects. The military's logic wasn't about ego or glory; it was genuinely about preventing the scenario where the Soviets strike first while America hesitates. Fear of asymmetric vulnerability drives defection.

pairs.back: Best mutual outcome — Cuban Missile Crisis resolved, both sides save face
You 'win' locally — but only if they don't retaliate (rarely true in repeated games)
Worst mutual outcome — nuclear war, price destruction, relationship breakdown
You cooperate, they defect — maximum individual loss, drives fear of going first

pairs.left: WWI trench soldiers seeing each other's faces
pairs.right: Making identities visible to enable cooperation

pairs.left: Military rotating units to prevent truces
Both sides secretly wanting the same outcome but not acting
pairs.right: The structure of the prisoner's dilemma

pairs.left: Public removal of Cuban missiles, quiet removal of Turkish ones
pairs.right: Removing audience cost so cooperation doesn't look like weakness

before.label: Trapped in the dilemma
before.text: I can see we'd both benefit from cooperation, but I can't risk going first because if they don't reciprocate, I'm the sucker. So I'll keep defecting and hope they blink first.

after.label: Breaking the structure
after.text: I'll name the game we're in, create a private channel to discuss without audience pressure, signal that this interaction will repeat, and make a small cooperative move first to test their response — while being ready to punish defection immediately.
